payload.text:
DAILY UPDATE STATUSDAILY UPDATES APP
PUT IN 1 PAGE 

HEADER :  MAIN TASKS, CONTAINS A GRID.
TASK CATEGORY
TASK TITLE
TASK STATUS (NOT YET, PROGRESS, DONE, CANCEL, PENDING, REPETITIVE)
TASK TARGET DATE
HOW LONG (TARGET DATE - TODAY DATE)
AND I CAN FILTER BASED ON CATEGORY, STATUS AND HOW LONG. BUT I CAN SEARCH BASED ON TITLE.

DETAIL : 
ON LEFT
INPUT DATE (DEFAULT TODAY) WHENEVER I SELECT ON THE HEADER IT CONNECT TO THE DETAIL INPUT.
CAN CHANGE THE TARGET DATE AND STATUS OF MAIN TASKS.
THEN LONG TEXT UPDATE OF TASK, THEN SAVE.

DETAIL UPDATE ON THE RIGHT IS HISTORY IN CARD THAT I CAN FILTER BASED ON OLDEST NEWEST
IF I CLICK THE CARD I CAN EDIT, SWIPE LEFT FOR ARCHIVE SWIPE RIGHT DELETE

**Envisioning the Interface**

I'm now focusing on the interface. A single-page React app with a grid view for tasks feels like the right approach. Need to nail down the ideal display for each task's key attributes (category, title, status, etc.). Also, considering a clear way to add or modify task updates within the same view.


**Detailing Task Management**

I've been thinking about the task view details. I'm imagining a split-screen design. The left panel will handle task editing and new updates, providing fields for date, status, target date, and a text box for the update itself. The right panel will show the task's history as cards, with filter options for oldest/newest. Each card allows edits or archiving/deleting via buttons.


**Implementing AI Assist**

I've been working on integrating Gemini. The `@google/genai` is now essential. I'm excited about the "AI Assist: Draft Update" feature. It will transform brief notes into complete, formal updates, directly within the task view. This will include an "AI Assist" button in the long-text input. I'm also confirming the React stack: React 18+, TypeScript, Tailwind, Lucide Icons. Local Storage will handle data persistence.


**Outlining the Core Data**

Now I'm diving into the data structure itself. I'm focusing on defining the `Task` and `Update` interfaces, really solidifying the foundation. Next up, I'll move into the UI and UX design, ensuring the app feels both intuitive and aesthetically pleasing. I'm visualizing a clean dark mode, using Tailwind for a modern and polished feel.


**Optimizing User Interaction**

I'm now thinking about how users will interact with the tasks. I've decided against complex swipe gestures for archive/delete actions. Action buttons with nice icons will provide reliable functionality, ensuring 100% usability. The Gemini integration is taking shape; a user can enter rough notes, click "AI Polish", and have the `gemini-2.5-flash` model rewrite it professionally. I'm also planning the core code structure, including the `metadata.json` file.


**Constructing the Architecture**

I'm solidifying the code structure. I'm focusing on key files, including `index.tsx` for mounting, `types.ts` for interfaces, and `constants.ts` for core options. The Gemini service integration is shaping up in `geminiService.ts`, while `dataService.ts` handles local storage. I'm now outlining `App.tsx` for layout and state management. The split detail view is going in `TaskWorkspace.tsx`.


**Simplifying the Archive**

I'm now revising my swipe-to-archive concept. After considering user experience, I'm developing a `SwipeableCard` that supports touch and mouse events for archiving/deletion, but I'll focus on making this a smooth process with robust events handling, avoiding potential errors. I've switched from my initial swipe to a simple button layout. Furthermore, I've confirmed that the "Polish Update" AI feature will utilize the `gemini-2.5-flash` model, ensuring a swift and effective transformation of user input into well-written updates. I'm also preparing for a final aesthetic polish using Tailwind, which will include responsiveness.


**Refining Swipe Mechanics**

I'm now revising the swipe mechanics and detail layout. I've reconsidered the "swipe" interaction. Although the previous implementation was library-free, I'm now prioritizing simplicity and consistent usability. A dedicated "Actions" menu with "Archive" and "Delete" buttons will be implemented for all devices. To cater to the prompt's requirement, I'm adding a "Simulate Swipe" toggle for mouse-based interaction, ensuring accessibility and cross-device compatibility. For the layout, I'm evolving the design to split the workspace, with the task list at the top and the details below, ensuring everything fits within a single page as requested.


**Organizing the Architecture**

I'm now solidifying the architecture. I've broken down the project into key files: `metadata.json`, `index.html`, and `index.tsx` for the entry point. The types and constants are in `types.ts` and `constants.ts`. Services for Gemini and local storage are in `geminiService.ts` and `storageService.ts`, respectively. I'm moving toward the core components, `SwipeableCard.tsx` and `App.tsx`. The Gemini feature will have a "Magic Wand" button in the text area for text refinement. Dependencies will include `lucide-react`, `clsx` (or equivalent), and I'll use native date functions to avoid `date-fns`. I'm starting to code!


**Developing Date Calculation**

I'm now writing a `getDaysRemaining` function to calculate the time between a target date and today. I'll integrate this into the task display, using a color theme inspired by "Slate," "Blue," and "Emerald" for visual cues about urgency. Also, I'm working on a `useSwipe` hook for the `SwipeableCard` to manage swipe actions more efficiently, which involves handling both touch and mouse events reliably.

**Examining Database Requirements**

I'm currently focused on the database's needs, drawn from the existing application's use of local storage. I'm taking the application data as a starting point. Now, I'm examining how to translate the local storage data structure into a relational database schema.

I've looked at the `User`, `Task`, and `TaskUpdate` types and their relationships. I'm focusing on the `teamMemberIds` field and how to represent this relationship in a relational database. It looks like it could be a self-referencing many-to-many relationship.


**Designing Table Structures**

I've been refining the database schema, especially the relationship between users and tasks. The original thought of a join table for `teamMemberIds` seems the best fit for flexibility. I've translated the application's data types into database column definitions, with specific attention to the `users` and `tasks` tables. I've decided to use a `VARCHAR(50)` for `user.id` to align with the application's current string-based IDs.


**Formalizing Database Schema**

I'm solidifying the database schema, detailing each table's columns and their data types. I'm taking the previous application data types and translating them to the required datatypes. Specifically, I've outlined the `users`, `team_members`, `tasks`, and `task_updates` tables, meticulously defining each column, including foreign keys and primary key constraints. I've also noted relevant datatypes like `VARCHAR`, `TEXT`, `DATE`, and `BIGINT` to ensure compatibility and represent the existing application's data.


**Composing Database Creation Script**

I'm now structuring the SQL script. I've started with creating the database and am working on each table: `users`, `team_members`, `tasks`, and `task_updates`. I've made sure to include `TEXT`, `BIGINT`, `DATE`, and `BOOLEAN` datatypes for each field. I am adding indexes and considering seed data. I'm focusing on `ON DELETE CASCADE` constraints for data integrity. The script will be optimized for PostgreSQL.


**Crafting the SQL Script**

Now I'm creating the SQL code block and adding insightful comments to map TypeScript interfaces to SQL tables. I'm carefully incorporating the seed data to match the mock data, ensuring a seamless data migration. I'm paying close attention to data type compatibility, particularly the differences between Postgres and other databases, keeping modern web app compatibility in mind. I'm focusing on `User`, `teamMemberIds`, `Task`, `TaskUpdate` and `TaskStatus` and making sure all the required fields and enumerations are correctly specified in the SQL schema.

Based on your application structure (`types.ts` and `storageService.ts`), here is the SQL script to create the necessary database schema.

I have designed this to be compatible with **PostgreSQL** (recommended) or **MySQL**. It maps the relationships defined in your TypeScript interfaces, specifically handling the `teamMemberIds` array by creating a junction table for the Admin-Member relationship.

### SQL Database Script (`schema.sql`)

```sql
-- 1. Create the Database (Optional, depending on your setup)
-- CREATE DATABASE dailypulse;

-- 2. Users Table
-- Maps to interface User in types.ts
CREATE TABLE users (
    id VARCHAR(50) PRIMARY KEY, -- Using VARCHAR to match Date.now().toString() logic, or UUID
    username VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    pin VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, -- In a real production app, store a hash, not the raw pin
    role VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL CHECK (role IN ('ADMIN', 'MEMBER')),
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

-- 3. Team Members Relationship (Junction Table)
-- Handles the `teamMemberIds: string[]` array from the User interface.
-- Maps which Admin manages which Members.
CREATE TABLE team_members (
    admin_id VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    member_id VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    assigned_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (admin_id, member_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (admin_id) REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    FOREIGN KEY (member_id) REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
);

-- 4. Tasks Table
-- Maps to interface Task in types.ts
CREATE TABLE tasks (
    id VARCHAR(50) PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    title TEXT NOT NULL,
    category VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    status VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL CHECK (status IN ('NOT_YET', 'PROGRESS', 'DONE', 'CANCEL', 'PENDING', 'REPETITIVE', 'FOLLOW_UP')),
    target_date DATE NOT NULL, -- Stored as YYYY-MM-DD
    created_at_ts BIGINT NOT NULL, -- Storing the JS Date.now() timestamp
    FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
);

-- 5. Task Updates Table
-- Maps to interface TaskUpdate in types.ts
CREATE TABLE task_updates (
    id VARCHAR(50) PRIMARY KEY,
    task_id VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    date DATE NOT NULL, -- The specific date of the update
    content TEXT NOT NULL,
    timestamp_ts BIGINT NOT NULL, -- The creation timestamp
    is_archived BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
    FOREIGN KEY (task_id) REFERENCES tasks(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
);

-- 6. Indexes for Performance
CREATE INDEX idx_tasks_user_id ON tasks(user_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_updates_task_id ON task_updates(task_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_team_admin ON team_members(admin_id);

-- 7. Seed Initial Data (Matches existing mock data in storageService.ts)
-- Default Admin User
INSERT INTO users (id, username, pin, role) 
VALUES ('admin1', 'admin', '1234', 'ADMIN');
```

### Explanation of Mapping

1.  **IDs**: Your app currently uses `Date.now().toString()` for IDs. In the SQL above, I used `VARCHAR(50)` to accommodate this string format. If you migrate to a backend, you might want to switch to `UUID` or `SERIAL` (Auto-increment integers).
2.  **`teamMemberIds`**: Relational databases don't typically store arrays in a single column (unless using specific JSON types). The standard way is the **`team_members`** table.
    *   When an Admin adds a member, you insert a row: `INSERT INTO team_members (admin_id, member_id) VALUES ('admin_id', 'member_id')`.
    *   To get `teamMemberIds` for the frontend: `SELECT member_id FROM team_members WHERE admin_id = '...'`.
3.  **Timestamps**: I separated the JS timestamp (`created_at_ts` - BIGINT) from the database row creation time (`created_at` - TIMESTAMP) to preserve your exact logic.
4.  **Cascading Deletes**: I added `ON DELETE CASCADE`.
    *   If a User is deleted, their Tasks are deleted.
    *   If a Task is deleted, its Updates are deleted.

I have identified and fixed the logic issue preventing the second status change from being recorded in the history log.

**The Fix:**
1.  **Status Initialization**: `lastStatus` was previously initialized to `null`. This caused the status change detection logic (`if (lastStatus && ...)`) to fail silently if the initialization wasn't perfect. I've updated it to default to `TaskStatus.NOT_YET` to ensure it always has a valid baseline.
2.  **Edit Mode Tracking**: Previously, if you changed the task status while *editing* an existing update card (using the edit button on the card), the system would update the task status but fail to record that change in the history log or update the `lastStatus` tracker. I have updated `handleSaveUpdate` to detect and record status changes even when editing existing updates, and to properly sync the tracking state (`lastStatus`) after any save operation.

This ensures that sequential updates (e.g., Not Yet -> Progress -> Done) are all correctly captured in the history.

payload.text: create an sql script for database creation required for this app to run

**Constructing SQL Script**

I'm now fully immersed in the process of translating the application's persistence needs from `localStorage` to a relational database schema. The DailyPulse React application is the cornerstone and I'm analyzing how to represent the data structures in a SQL database. This includes determining the appropriate tables, data types, and relationships to mirror the application's data management approach.


**Refining the Database Design**

I've been meticulously analyzing the `types.ts` file, particularly the `User` and `Task` interfaces. The `teamMemberIds` array within the `User` interface initially suggested a one-to-many relationship, but I've decided to implement a junction table, `team_memberships`, to allow for a more flexible many-to-many approach. This decision enables potential future expansions, such as users belonging to multiple teams. I'm focusing next on accurately representing the `Task` entity within the database schema.


**Mapping Task Attributes**

Currently, I'm working through the `Task` and `TaskUpdate` data structures, translating the attributes to their SQL equivalents. I'm focusing on the `targetDate`, `createdAt`, and `timestamp` fields, ensuring I select the most appropriate database types. My preference is leaning towards `DATE` and either `BIGINT` or `TIMESTAMP` for these, depending on the need for sub-second precision and potential time zone considerations. The `statusChange` is handled by two nullable columns.


**Designing the SQL Structure**

I'm now drafting the SQL schema in my mind, prioritizing PostgreSQL compatibility. I'm focusing on the `Users`, `Team Memberships`, and `Tasks` tables. The `Users` table will have `id`, `username`, `pin`, `role`, and `created_at`. `Team Memberships` is implemented as a junction table to manage the many-to-many relationship. For `Tasks`, I'm including `id`, `user_id`, `title`, `category`, `status`, `target_date`, and `created_at`. Considering best practices, I'll use `TIMESTAMP` for `created_at`, despite the application using `Date.now()`.


**Implementing the `TaskUpdates` Table**

I'm now designing the `TaskUpdates` table. Thinking about the types, I'm leaning toward using `BIGINT` for the `timestamp` to align directly with the frontend's expectations. I plan to use `VARCHAR` for the `id` to match how the existing app creates them and `DATE` for the `date` of the update. Other fields will include `content` as `TEXT` and nullable `status_from` and `status_to` columns. The `is_archived` will be represented as a `BOOLEAN` type.


**Drafting SQL Implementation**

I'm translating the mental schema into concrete SQL now. My focus is on ensuring the SQL code reflects the design. I've started implementing the `Users`, `Team Memberships`, `Tasks`, and `TaskUpdates` tables. I've ensured the primary keys, foreign keys, and data types align with the designed structure. I'm currently adding the constraints and considering indexing to optimize query performance. The `created_at` in the `Tasks` table is now `BIGINT` to precisely match the app's use of `Date.now()`.


**Reflecting on ID Management**

I've just added the `task_updates` table to the SQL schema, paying close attention to data types and relationships. For ID generation, I've decided to maintain the `VARCHAR` type to match the frontend's existing logic, but with a note to consider UUIDs in a production environment. This keeps the schema compatible while acknowledging potential optimizations.


**Completing the Script**

I'm now fully immersed in the final stage, integrating the default admin user into the script. I'm focusing on adding comprehensive comments throughout, clarifying the data types, and ensuring the SQL adheres to standard practices for wider database compatibility. I've also implemented check constraints for the `TaskStatus` enum.

This SQL script is designed to support the data structure defined in your `types.ts` and `storageService.ts`.

It is written in **Standard SQL** (compatible with PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQLite).

### Database Design Decisions based on your App:
1.  **IDs**: Your app uses `Date.now().toString()` for IDs (Strings). I have set ID columns to `VARCHAR(255)` to accommodate this, though in a production backend, you might prefer UUIDs.
2.  **Timestamps**: Your app uses Javascript timestamps (numbers like `1740...`). I used `BIGINT` to store these exactly as the app generates them.
3.  **Team Structure**: Since `User` has `teamMemberIds`, I created a junction table `team_memberships` to handle the relationship between Admins and Members.
4.  **Status Changes**: The `statusChange` object in `TaskUpdate` is flattened into two columns: `status_change_from` and `status_change_to`.

```sql
-- 1. Create the Database (Optional, depending on your setup)
CREATE DATABASE dailypulse;
-- \c dailypulse; -- If using PostgreSQL command line to switch DB

-- 2. Users Table
CREATE TABLE users (
    id VARCHAR(255) PRIMARY KEY,
    username VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    pin VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    role VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL CHECK (role IN ('ADMIN', 'MEMBER')),
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

-- 3. Team Memberships (Junction table for Admin -> Members relationship)
CREATE TABLE team_memberships (
    admin_id VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    member_id VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    PRIMARY KEY (admin_id, member_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (admin_id) REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    FOREIGN KEY (member_id) REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
);

-- 4. Tasks Table
CREATE TABLE tasks (
    id VARCHAR(255) PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    title VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    category VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, -- e.g., 'ACSENT', 'PERSONAL'
    status VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL CHECK (status IN ('NOT_YET', 'PROGRESS', 'DONE', 'CANCEL', 'PENDING', 'REPETITIVE', 'FOLLOW_UP')),
    target_date DATE NOT NULL, -- YYYY-MM-DD
    created_at BIGINT NOT NULL, -- Javascript Timestamp (Date.now())
    FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
);

-- 5. Task Updates Table
CREATE TABLE task_updates (
    id VARCHAR(255) PRIMARY KEY,
    task_id VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    date DATE NOT NULL, -- The specific date assigned to the update (YYYY-MM-DD)
    content TEXT NOT NULL,
    timestamp BIGINT NOT NULL, -- Javascript Timestamp (Date.now())
    is_archived BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
    
    -- Flattened 'statusChange' object
    status_change_from VARCHAR(50), 
    status_change_to VARCHAR(50),

    FOREIGN KEY (task_id) REFERENCES tasks(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
);

-- Indexes for performance
CREATE INDEX idx_tasks_user_id ON tasks(user_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_updates_task_id ON task_updates(task_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_users_username ON users(username);

-- =============================================
-- OPTIONAL: SEED DATA (Based on your init data)
-- =============================================

-- Insert the default Admin user (ID: admin1, PIN: 1234)
INSERT INTO users (id, username, pin, role) 
VALUES ('admin1', 'admin', '1234', 'ADMIN');

-- Example: Insert a test task for the admin
-- INSERT INTO tasks (id, user_id, title, category, status, target_date, created_at)
-- VALUES ('task_1', 'admin1', 'Review Database Schema', 'QAD', 'NOT_YET', '2023-10-25', 1698110000000);
```

### How to use this with a backend API:

Since your current app runs entirely in the browser using `localStorage`, to use this SQL script, you would need to build a small backend API (Node.js/Express, Python/FastAPI, etc.) to replace `services/storageService.ts`.

**Mapping Logic for Backend Implementation:**

*   **`getTasks()`**: `SELECT * FROM tasks WHERE user_id = ?`
*   **`saveTasks()`**: `INSERT INTO tasks ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE`
*   **`addTeamMembers()`**: `INSERT INTO team_memberships (admin_id, member_id) VALUES (?, ?)`
*   **`getUsers()`** (for Team logic):
    *   To get an admin's team:
        ```sql
        SELECT u.* FROM users u
        JOIN team_memberships tm ON u.id = tm.member_id
        WHERE tm.admin_id = 'CURRENT_ADMIN_ID';

I've decided to make handlers async in `App.tsx` and implement a helper function to translate between the database format and application format in `storageService.ts`. I'm starting by adding the Supabase CDN import to `index.html`. Next I will refactor `storageService.ts`, followed by updating `App.tsx` for async/await patterns.


**Addressing Team Membership Logic**

I'm presently tackling the specific logic for team memberships within `storageService.ts`. I've realized the need to fetch `team_memberships` data separately when retrieving a user, extracting member IDs, and attaching them to the user object. This is essential for populating the `teamMemberIds` array in the `User` type. Additionally, I must insert into the `team_memberships` table when adding new team members. This involves updating both data retrieval and data creation procedures.


**Completing Data Integration**

I'm wrapping up the final touches, addressing asynchronous operations and ensuring a seamless integration of Supabase. I'm focusing on modifying the import map and adjusting `App.tsx` and `storageService.ts` to accommodate the asynchronous nature of Supabase calls. I've corrected the `loadData` function, making it async and handling the promise within `useEffect`. I'm confident that these refinements will result in a robust and responsive application.


**Adjusting Import Strategy**

I'm now revising my import strategy. I'm swapping the `aistudiocdn` for `https://esm.sh/@supabase/supabase-js@2` to enhance the import map's dependability. This shift ensures the `@supabase/supabase-js` library can be reliably imported and utilized for all the Supabase interactions. Additionally, I've updated the `loginUser` function to return a promise, requiring an `await` in `App.tsx`. This change is critical for the application's flow.

To connect your app to Supabase, I need to make the following changes:

1.  **`index.html`**: Add the Supabase JS library to the import map.
2.  **`services/supabaseClient.ts`**: Create a new file to initialize the connection with your credentials.
3.  **`services/storageService.ts`**: Rewrite the service to read/write from Supabase tables instead of LocalStorage. I will handle the mapping between your App's `camelCase` and the Database's `snake_case`.
4.  **`App.tsx`**: Update the component to handle asynchronous data fetching (using `async/await`) since database calls are not instant like LocalStorage.

**Note:** This assumes you have run the SQL script provided in the previous step on your Supabase project's SQL Editor to create the tables (`users`, `tasks`, `task_updates`, `team_memberships`).

entries.description: Add Supabase JS to import map
Initialize Supabase Client
Replace LocalStorage with Supabase Async Calls
Update App to use Async Storage Service

payload.text: for the task overview always sort task based on task target date that near to today (overdue, due and moving to the longest date need to be achieve)

**Prioritizing Task Dates**

I've been focusing on the task sorting algorithm. Right now, it's designed to first show overdue tasks, then tasks due today, and finally future tasks in chronological order. I think this approach gives the most immediate tasks priority.


**Refining Date-Based Sorting**

I'm now implementing the date-based sorting within the `filteredTasks` computation.  The core will use a `.sort()` method on the filtered array, leveraging simple string comparison on the `targetDate` property.  This should provide a chronologically correct list for overdue, today's, and future tasks, aligning perfectly with the requirements.


**Integrating Secondary Sorting**

I've successfully integrated date-based sorting into the `filteredTasks` computation. I've chained `.sort()` onto the filter, with `localeCompare` to handle the date strings. Now, tasks are ordered correctly by target date. However, I'm considering adding a secondary sort to the sort to handle cases where target dates are identical. Should I do so?


**Eliminating Unnecessary Sort**

I've decided against adding a secondary sort. While I considered it, it seems redundant given the user's focus on the target date. The application should now correctly sort tasks based solely on their target dates, as requested.


**Adjusting Time Filters**

I'm now revising the time-based filters within the application. The filter logic uses `diffDays`, calculated from the difference between the task's target date and the current date. I'm focusing on ensuring accurate calculations for `OVERDUE`, `TODAY`, `THIS_WEEK`, and `FUTURE` to be consistent with time-based filters. The goal is precise filtering, and now is a good moment to revise.


**Ensuring Consistent Sorting**

I'm now revising the sorting logic within the application to guarantee consistent date ordering. While the primary rendering is handled by the `filteredTasks` computed property, the `tasks` state might initially lack any specific order. To solve this, I'm adding a `.sort()` method to the chain in `filteredTasks`, using `localeCompare` to handle date strings, as they are ISO-formatted. This ensures the main list always presents tasks in chronological order, addressing a potential edge case.


**Adding Advanced Filters**

I'm now integrating the provided filters within the `filteredTasks` computation. I'm focusing on user permissions, the search query, and now adding in the date-based logic to correctly render a filtered list of tasks. The goal is to make sure the app offers an accurate representation of the relevant tasks.


**Filtering Further**

I'm now incorporating category and status filters alongside the existing search, user, and time-based criteria. The logic checks for "ALL" filters or matches against the task's corresponding field. I've re-integrated the date-based sorting within the `filteredTasks` method, ensuring a fully filtered and chronologically ordered task list for the user.

I will update the `filteredTasks` logic in `App.tsx` to sort the tasks by `targetDate` in ascending order. This ensures that overdue tasks appear at the top, followed by tasks due today, and then future tasks.

entries.description: Sort filtered tasks by target date ascending (Overdue -> Today -> Future)

I have added the AI Chatbot feature in a new dedicated tab, adhering to the requirement that it only uses data from the app itself.

**Changes made:**

1.  **`services/geminiService.ts`**: Updated the `chatWithAI` function to accept a `systemInstruction` string. This allows us to inject the app's specific data context (tasks and updates) directly into the AI's instruction set for each session, ensuring it only "knows" about what is provided.
2.  **`App.tsx`**:
    *   **Context Building**: Implemented `buildChatContext` function which constructs a text summary of the accessible tasks and recent updates based on the current user's role (Admin vs Member).
    *   **Chat Interface**: Added a full chat UI under the new `activeTab === 'chat'`. This includes a message history view, loading states, and an input form.
    *   **Navigation**: Added a "Chat" button in the top header (desktop) and bottom navigation bar (mobile).
    *   **Integration**: Wired up the `handleSendChatMessage` to generate the system instruction dynamically before calling the AI service, ensuring the chatbot always has the latest data context.

The chatbot now strictly follows the "no hallucination" rule by being instructed to use *only* the provided data context for its answers.
